blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَعْطَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْكَوْثَرَ (١)
Indeed We have given you abundance. (1)
فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَٱنْحَرْ (٢)
So pray to your Lord, and sacrifice [the sacrificial camel]. (2)
إِنَّ شَانِئَكَ هُوَ ٱلْأَبْتَرُ (٣)
Indeed it is your enemy who is without posterity. (3)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Kawthar"

The popular view is that this Surah was revealed in "Mecca," though some have suggested the possibility of it being Medinan. It has also been proposed that this Surah was revealed twice—once in Mecca and once in Medina—but the narrations regarding the occasion of its revelation support the popular view that it is Meccan.

Regarding the occasion of revelation, we read: "As ibn Wa'il," who was among the leaders of the polytheists, met the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) as he was exiting Al-Masjid al-Haram and spoke with him for a while. A group of Quraysh leaders were sitting in the mosque observing this scene from afar. When As ibn Wa'il entered the mosque, they asked him: "With whom were you speaking?" He replied: "With that 'abtar' man!" He chose this expression because Abdullah, the son of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), had passed away, and the Arabs used to call someone who had no son "abtar" (meaning without offspring). Thus, the Quraysh had chosen this name for the Prophet after the death of his son. (The above Surah was revealed, giving the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) the glad tidings of abundant blessings and "Kawthar," while calling his enemies "abtar") [^1]. To clarify: the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had two sons from the Lady of Islam, Khadijah: one was "Qasim" and the other "Tahir" (who was also called Abdullah). Both passed away in Mecca, leaving the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) without a male child. This matter opened the tongues of the ill-wishers of Quraysh, and they chose the word "abtar" for him [^1]. According to their custom, they placed extraordinary importance on male children, considering them the continuers of the father's programs. After this incident, they thought that with the passing of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), his mission would cease due to the lack of male offspring, and they were joyful.

The Holy Qur'an was revealed and responded to them in a miraculous manner in this Surah, informing them that his enemies would be the ones who are "abtar," and that the program of Islam and the Qur'an would never be cut off. The glad tidings given in this Surah were, on one hand, a blow to the hopes of the enemies of Islam, and on the other, a consolation to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), whose pure heart had become saddened and distressed after hearing this ugly title and the conspiracies of the enemies.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, a hadith (from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله) states:

"Whoever recites it, Allah will quench his thirst from the rivers of Paradise, and will grant him a reward equal to the number of every sacrifice that the servants of God offer on the Day of Eid (al-Adha), as well as the sacrifices offered by the People of the Book and the polytheists." [^2] The name of this Surah (Kawthar) is taken from its first verse.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 549. [^1]: The Holy Prophet had another male child named "Ibrahim" from "Mariyah al-Qibtiyyah" in Medina in the eighth year of the Hijra, but coincidentally, he also passed away before reaching the age of two, and his death grieved the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) pure heart. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 548.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِنَّا أَعْطَيْناكَ الْكَوْثَرَ (1) فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَ انْحَرْ (2) إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ (3)

Commentary

We Have Given You Abundant Good

The address throughout this Surah is to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) (similar to Surah Ad-Duha and Surah Al-Inshirah), and one of the major objectives of all three Surahs is to console him in the face of a multitude of painful events and the repeated taunts of the enemies.

First, He says: "Indeed, We have granted you Al-Kawthar" (إِنَّا أَعْطَيْناكَ الْكَوْثَرَ).

"Kawthar" is a description derived from "kathrah" (abundance), meaning "abundant good and blessing," and generous individuals are also referred to as "Kawthar."

Regarding what is meant by "Kawthar" here, a narration states that when this Surah was revealed, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) ascended the pulpit and recited it. The companions asked: "What is this that Allah has granted you?" He said: "It is a river in Paradise, whiter than milk and clearer than a (crystal) goblet; on its two sides are domes of pearls and rubies..." [^1]. In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "Kawthar is a river in Paradise that Allah granted to His Prophet in exchange for his son (Abdullah, who passed away during his lifetime)."

Some have also said it refers to the "Pond of Kawthar" (Hawd al-Kawthar) which belongs to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and from which the believers will quench their thirst upon entering Paradise [^2]. Some have interpreted it as Prophethood, others as the Qur'an, some as the abundance of companions and followers, and some as the abundance of children and progeny, all of whom came from the lineage of his daughter Fatima al-Zahra (عليها السلام). They increased to such an extent that they are beyond counting and remain as reminders of the Prophet's existence until the Day of Resurrection. Some have also interpreted it as "Intercession" (Shafa'ah) and cited a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in this regard [^3]. Fakhr al-Razi mentioned fifteen opinions in the interpretation of "Kawthar," but it appears that most of these are expressions of clear instances of this broad and expansive concept. As we mentioned, "Kawthar" means "abundant good and plentiful blessing," and we know that the Great Allah bestowed many abundant blessings upon the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), each of which mentioned above is one of its clear manifestations, and it has many other manifestations that could be mentioned as specific interpretations of the verse.

In any case, all divine favors upon the person of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in all fields—including his victories in battles over enemies, and even the scholars of his Ummah who in every age and time guard the flaming torch of the Qur'an and Islam and carry it to every corner of the world—are all included in this "abundant good."

It should not be forgotten that Allah said this to His Prophet at a time when the signs of this "abundant good" had not yet appeared. This was news of the near and distant future—miraculous news that demonstrated the truthfulness of the Messenger's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call.

This great blessing and abundant good require great gratitude, although the gratitude of a creature can never fulfill the right of the Creator's blessing; rather, the success to give thanks is itself another blessing from Him. Therefore, He says: "So pray to your Lord and sacrifice" (فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَ انْحَرْ). Yes, He is the Giver of the blessing; therefore, prayer, worship, and sacrifice—which is also a form of worship—make no sense for anyone other than Him, especially considering the concept of "Rabb" (Lord), which signifies the continuity of blessings and the providence and Lordship of the Creator.

In short, "worship," whether in the form of prayer or sacrifice, belongs exclusively to the "Lord" and the Benefactor, and He is exclusively the Pure Essence of God.

This is in contrast to the actions of the polytheists who prostrated and sacrificed to idols while attributing their blessings to God! In any case, the expression "for your Lord" (li-Rabbika) is clear evidence of the necessity of the intention of proximity (qurbah) in acts of worship.

Many commentators believe that the meaning is the prayer of the Day of Eid al-Adha and the sacrifice on that same day, but apparently, the concept of the verse is general and broad, even if the Eid prayer and sacrifice are among its clearest examples.

The expression "wanhar" is from the root "nahr," which is specifically used for slaughtering camels. This is perhaps because among sacrifices, the camel held the greatest importance and the early Muslims were very fond of it; sacrificing a camel was not possible without selflessness and sacrifice.

Two other interpretations for the above verse are mentioned here: 1. The phrase "wanhar" refers to facing the Qibla during prayer, as the root "nahr" means the throat area, and the Arabs subsequently used it to mean "facing anything." Thus they say "manaziluna tatanahar," meaning "our houses face one another." 2. It refers to raising the hands during the Takbir and bringing them opposite the throat and face. In a hadith, we read: "When this Surah was revealed, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked Gabriel: 'What is this nahirah [^1] that my Lord has commanded me to do?'"

[^1]: Nahirah can mean the last day of the month, as on that day one goes to meet the new month. Some have taken it to mean the last night and day of the month. Thus, the meaning of the narration would be: "What is this welcoming of the coming month that God has commanded me?" Hence Gabriel said, "It is not nahirah."

"Gabriel said: 'This is not nahirah; rather, Allah commands you that when you enter prayer, raise your hands during the Takbir, and also when you bow (ruku'), or raise your head from bowing, or when you prostrate, for our prayer and the prayer of the angels in the seven heavens is like this. Everything has an adornment, and the adornment of prayer is raising the hands at every Takbir.'" [^1] In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that in interpreting this verse, he gestured with his blessed hand and said: "The meaning is that you raise your hands like this at the beginning of the prayer so that the palms face the Qibla." [^2] However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all, as the intent is to negate the practices of the idolaters who performed worship and sacrifice for other than God. Nevertheless, there is no obstacle to combining all the meanings and narrations received on this subject, especially since numerous narrations regarding raising the hands during the Takbirs have been narrated in both Shi'a and Sunni books. Thus, the verse has a comprehensive meaning that includes these as well.

In the final verse of this Surah, referring to the label the leaders of polytheism attributed to him, He says: "You are not abtar and without offspring; your enemy is the one who is abtar!" (إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ). "Shani'" is from the root "shana'an," meaning enmity, malice, and ill-temper, and "shani'" is one who possesses this trait.

[^1] & [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 550.

It is noteworthy that "abtar" originally means an "animal with a cut tail" [^1]. The choice of this expression by the enemies of Islam was for the purpose of insult and humiliation, and the term "shani'" reflects the reality that in their enmity, they did not observe even the slightest etiquette; that is, their hostility was mixed with cruelty and vileness. In reality, the Qur'an says: "This title belongs to yourselves, not to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)."

On the other hand, as mentioned regarding the occasion of revelation, the Quraysh were waiting for the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) death and the dismantling of the structure of Islam, as they said he was without offspring. The Qur'an says: "You are not without offspring; your enemies are the ones without offspring!"

Key Points

1- "Fatima (عليه السلام)" and "Kawthar"

We said that "Kawthar" has a comprehensive and broad meaning, which is "abundant good," and its instances are many. However, many great Shi'a scholars have considered one of its clearest manifestations to be the blessed existence of "Fatima al-Zahra" (peace be upon her). This is because the occasion of revelation says they accused the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) of being without offspring; the Qur'an, while negating their words, says: "We have given you Kawthar."

From this expression, it is inferred that this "abundant good" is Fatima al-Zahra (عليه السلام), because the lineage and progeny of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) spread throughout the world through this noble daughter—a lineage that was not only the physical children of the Prophet but also preserved his religion and all the values of Islam and conveyed them to future generations. Not only the Infallible Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), who have their own special status, but thousands upon thousands of the children of Fatima (عليه السلام) spread across the world, among whom were great scholars, writers, jurists, traditionists, commentators of high rank, and great commanders who strove with self-sacrifice to preserve the religion of Islam.

Here we encounter an interesting discussion by Fakhr al-Razi, who says among the various interpretations of Kawthar: "The third view is that this Surah was revealed as a rebuttal to those who criticized the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) for the lack of male children. Therefore, the meaning of the Surah is that Allah gives him a lineage that will remain throughout time. See how many of the Ahl al-Bayt they martyred, yet the world is full of them, while from the Umayyads (who were the enemies of Islam) no one noteworthy remains in the world. Then look and see how many great scholars are among them, such as al-Baqir, al-Sadiq, al-Rida, and al-Nafs al-Zakiyyah." [^1] [^2]

2- The Miracle of this Surah

This Surah actually contains three great prophecies: on one hand, it promises the granting of "abundant good" to the Prophet (although "a'tainaka" is in the past tense, it may be of the type of a certain future event expressed in the past form), and this abundant good includes all the victories and successes that later came to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), which were not predictable at the time of this Surah's revelation in Mecca.

On the other hand, it informs that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) will not be without offspring; rather, his lineage and family will exist abundantly in the world.

Thirdly, it informs that his enemies will be "abtar" and without offspring. This prophecy was also fulfilled, and his enemies were so scattered that today no trace of them remains, while clans such as the "Umayyads" and "Abbasids" who rose to oppose the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his children once had such a population that their families and children were beyond counting, but today, if anything remains of them, it is never recognized.

3- Why the Plural Pronoun for God?

It is noteworthy that here and in many other verses of the Holy Qur'an, Allah refers to Himself using the "first person plural" (We), saying: "We" granted you Kawthar! This expression and its like are used to convey greatness and power, for when the great speak of themselves, they report not only of themselves but also of their agents, and this is a metaphor for power, majesty, and the existence of those who obey commands.

In the verse under discussion, the word "inna" (Indeed We) is another emphasis on this meaning, and the expression "a'tainaka" (We granted you) rather than "ataynaka" (We gave you) is evidence that Allah has bestowed and gifted "Kawthar" to him. This is a great glad tiding to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) so that his blessed heart would not be pained by the nonsense of the enemies, and so that no weakness or lethargy would find its way into his iron will, knowing that his support is the God who is the source of all goodness and has placed abundant good at his disposal.

[^1]: "Al-Nafs al-Zakiyyah" is a title for "Muhammad ibn Abdullah," the son of "Imam Hasan al-Mujtaba" (عليه السلام), who was martyred at the hands of Mansur al-Dawaniqi in the year 145 AH. [^2]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 32, p. 124. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 548.

O Allah! Do not deprive us of the blessings of this "abundant good" which You bestowed upon Your Prophet. O Lord! You know that we love him and his pure progeny from the bottom of our hearts; resurrect us among them. O God! His greatness and his religion are immense; increase this greatness, honor, and glory day by day.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Kawthar.
